serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mouth-Watering Chicken is approximately 150 g, representing a standard cold appetizer or side dish portion as served in Sichuan restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked chicken (stewed/poached, meat and skin) as the primary anchor, adjusted upward for fat and sodium to account for the chili oil dressing and soy sauce component characteristic of Mouth-Watering Chicken. Saturated fat estimated from USDA chicken fat composition data. The input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and retained where consistent. Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences source cited in input is not on the approved source list and has been replaced.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mouth-Watering Chicken. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dishes. The dish contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from soy sauce and sugar in the dressing. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6. Vinegar and chili components may further attenuate glycemic response. Estimation basis: peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (550 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce and chili oil dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and consider requesting reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 150 g serving delivers approximately 825 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mouth-Watering Chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load and high protein content; however, caloric density from chili oil should be monitored in the context of weight management goals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (3.8 g per 100 g) and high sodium (550 mg per 100 g) from chili oil and soy sauce dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, limiting portion size and sauce intake to manage sodium and saturated fat exposure, while benefiting from the lean protein content of the poached chicken.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds in ginger, garlic, and chili; potential nightshade sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and garlic in the dressing may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits; however, individuals sensitive to nightshades should be cautious with chili and Sichuan peppercorn components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mouth-Watering Chicken (口水鸡, Kǒushuǐ jī) is a classic Sichuan cold appetizer originating from Southwest China. The dish is prepared by poaching whole or bone-in chicken until just cooked through, then chilling it before slicing into bite-sized pieces. The cold chicken is served submerged in a complex, aromatic dressing made from chili oil, Sichuan peppercorns, soy sauce, black vinegar, garlic, ginger, and a small amount of sugar. It is garnished with toasted sesame seeds, crushed peanuts, and fresh cilantro. The flavor profile is a hallmark of Sichuan cuisine: simultaneously numbing (má) from the Sichuan peppercorns, spicy (là) from the chili oil, savory from the soy sauce, and slightly tangy from the black vinegar. The texture contrasts tender, silky cold chicken with the crunch of peanuts and sesame. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (18.5 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (14.5 g per 100 g), primarily from the chili oil dressing. Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.5 g per 100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated at 550 mg per 100 g due to the soy sauce component, which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 15), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. A standard serving is approximately 150 g.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked poached chicken, adjusted for the chili oil and soy sauce dressing component. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific Sichuan cold chicken preparation. The adaptation is well-reasoned and cross-checked against the AI-generated input values, which are broadly consistent with the USDA anchor. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature.
notes: water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for this specific Sichuan cold chicken preparation with chili oil dressing is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17. Saturated fat (3.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.5 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g), and cholesterol (75 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked chicken (stewed, meat and skin) adjusted for chili oil contribution. Trans fat is 0.0 g (confirmed negligible for this preparation type per USDA data). The original input cited the Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences as the nutrient source; this organization is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary anchor. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical 150 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
